title: The Arms and the Impulse to Push Away or Pull Close
researcherTopic: Eastern philosophy for Western minds, the wisdom of insecurity
metaDescription: Complex trauma creates a war in the body. We explore why your arms want to both push away and pull close, and how the path to healing lies not in choosing one, but in learning to inhabit the tension between them.
imageDescription: Warm, golden light illuminates a pair of hands, one gently holding the other's wrist in a gesture that is both supportive and still. The focus is soft, suggesting a moment of quiet reconciliation between the impulse to retreat and the desire to connect. The background is a simple, uncluttered space, emphasizing peace and internal focus.
htmlContent:
The hallway light flickers, a tiny, almost imperceptible pulse against the beige wall, and you find yourself standing in the doorway again. A friend has just left, their laughter still a faint echo in the air, and your arms hang at your sides like foreign objects, heavy and useless. one hand wants to flutter up, to wave them back for one more moment of connection, while the other feels an almost gravitational pull to reach for the deadbolt and slide it firmly into place. Your shoulders are a territory of tension, a silent, internal battleground where the genuine human need to reach out and the deeply learned instinct to pull back are locked in a stalemate, leaving you stranded in the excruciating space between them.

This is not a fleeting moment of social awkwardness; it is the physical embodiment of a nervous system fractured by experience. For those of us who have navigated the labyrinth of complex trauma, the arms cease to be simple appendages for carrying and holding. They are the anatomical expression of our deepest, most conflicted impulses. The internal war between reaching for connection and bracing for impact is not a metaphor; it is a physiological reality, a conversation happening in the language of muscle and nerve, telling the story of a biological imperative for closeness that has been repeatedly, and painfully, overwritten by the learned necessity of self-preservation.

The Primal Grammar of the Arms

Long before we had language to negotiate our needs, we had a more primal grammar, one written in the subtle choreography of the body. Our nervous system, particularly what researchers call the social engagement system, uses the face, the voice, and the arms to constantly send and receive signals of safety or danger. A soft gaze, a gentle tone, an open palm... They can offer, receive, protect, and embrace, conducting a complex diplomacy that shapes our every interaction. We learn this grammar in the cradle, in the way we are held or not held, in the arms that reached for us with tenderness or with tension.

Developmental trauma, however, fundamentally scrambles this innate grammar. When the very arms that are supposed to represent safety are also the source of neglect, unpredictability, or outright harm, the nervous system is thrown into a state of significant and lasting confusion. The tender, vulnerable impulse to reach for a caregiver becomes neurologically entangled with the terror of the threat response. That becomes not a conscious thought or a memory that can be easily recalled; it is a procedural memory, a lesson learned in the tissue and encoded in the autonomic response. The result is a body that lives in a state of perpetual paradox, where the drive to connect is simultaneously experienced as a life-threatening risk. We explore this unconscious scanning for danger in our article on the hidden sense of neuroception, a process that happens far below the threshold of conscious thought, keeping the body on high alert even in moments of relative peace.

The Felt Sense of Pushing Away

For many, this internal conflict shows up as a dominant pattern of “pushing away.” It turns out to be not a decision made in the cognitive mind but a felt sense, a physiological posture of defense that becomes a default way of being in the world. It lives in the body as chronic tension across the upper back and shoulders, a subtle but persistent bracing in the forearms, the hands often unconsciously curled into loose fists. This defensive shield is forged in the fires of past betrayals, and it remains long after the immediate threat has passed, an invisible armor that keeps the world, and everyone in it, at a managed distance.

I've sat with hundreds of people who describe this feeling as an invisible wall that descends without their permission, leaving them feeling genuinely isolated even in moments when they desperately crave closeness. They speak of a mysterious force that rises up from within, compelling them to find fault, to create distance, to sabotage the very connection they long for. It is a lonely and often confusing experience, to want love and to be physically incapable of letting it in, to feel the impulse to soften and yet find the muscles contracting in defiance. The body is simply running an old program, one that equates openness with danger, and it will continue to do so until it receives new information, not through words, but through lived, felt experience.

The Agonizing Gravity of Pulling Close

This pattern often leads to a striking loss of self within relationships. The focus is so intensely on the other - their moods, their needs, their desires ~ that one’s own internal world becomes a distant echo. The body learns to attune outward at the expense of attuning inward. We find ourselves reenacting these dynamics, pulling others close not to truly see them or be seen by them, but to use them as a shield against the primal terror of aloneness. It is a strategy that promises safety but ultimately delivers a deeper form of isolation, for when we are not connected to ourselves, we cannot truly connect with another.

The Wisdom of Insecurity in a Traumatized Body

The ceaseless dance of the arms, caught in the push and pull, is a perfect illustration of this principle. The impulse to push away is an attempt to secure the self by creating a boundary, a fortress against potential harm. The impulse to pull close is an attempt to secure the self by merging with another, by eliminating the terrifying space of separation. Both are strategies aimed at eradicating the feeling of insecurity. The arms are trying to solve a problem externally that can only be addressed internally. The path forward does not lie in strengthening the walls or tightening the grasp, but in turning toward the raw, uncomfortable, and deeply insecure sensations at the heart of the conflict. This echoes the principles we discuss in letting go of the story and returning to the body, where the work shifts from trying to fix the past to learning to inhabit the present.

The Architecture of a True Reach

So what does a healthy, integrated “reach” actually look like? How do we begin to build the architecture for a connection that is born not of fear, but of genuine presence? The answer, paradoxically, is that we do not start with the reach at all. The work is not to force the arms to open or to resist the urge to cling, but to cultivate the internal conditions of safety that allow the social engagement system to naturally, organically come back online. What we find here is the slow, patient, and deeply compassionate work of teaching the body, through direct sensation, that it is safe *now*, in this moment, regardless of what happened in the past.

Somatic therapies, whose efficacy is increasingly validated by studies found on platforms like NCBI, offer a powerful map for this road. They introduce concepts like “pendulation,” the practice of gently and deliberately guiding one’s attention between a sensation of contraction or activation (the push/pull tension) and even a tiny, fleeting sensation of ease or expansion somewhere else in the body. It could be the feeling of warmth in the hands, the solidness of the feet on the floor, or the gentle rhythm of the breath in the belly. It is in this gentle oscillation that the system learns, bit by bit, that activation is not a permanent state and that a return to ease is always possible. A true reach, then, is not an act of will, but the natural flowering of a nervous system that has rediscovered its own inherent capacity for regulation and connection.

From Information to Integration

It is possible to read all of this and feel a sense of resonance, a flicker of recognition in the mind. You might collect these concepts, arranging them neatly in your intellectual understanding of yourself. You can learn the names of the theories, trace the logic of the trauma response, and become an expert in the cartography of your own wounds. The turning point we seek does not happen at the level of information; it happens at the level of integration. It occurs when these ideas move from being abstract knowledge in the head to a lived, felt reality in the body.

The question, then, is not how you can stop the pushing and pulling. The question is: are you willing to feel the exquisite tension of both impulses at once, and stay right there, in the undecided center of your own life, long enough for a third way to be born?

Not what you expected, right?

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do my arms feel tense and sore all the time, even when I'm not working out?

This chronic tension is often the physical echo of a nervous system perpetually on guard. In the context of complex trauma, the body maintains a low-level state of readiness for a fight-or-flight response that never gets completed. Your muscles, particularly in the shoulders and arms, are holding the energy of a protective action - like pushing away a threat - that was initiated long ago and never resolved, resulting in persistent soreness and fatigue.

Is it possible to want someone close and want them to go away at the same time?

Absolutely. This significant ambivalence is a hallmark of a disorganized attachment system, which is frequently the outcome of developmental trauma. Your system is caught between two equally powerful and opposing survival drives.

How can I start to feel safe in my body again?

The path to reclaiming a sense of safety begins with shifting your focus from the large, abstract, and often overwhelming goal of "feeling safe" to the small, tangible, and achievable practice of noticing moments of "less danger." Instead of trying to force a feeling of calm, you can gently guide your attention to neutral or even pleasant physical sensations. Notice the solid feeling of your feet on the ground, the texture of the fabric of your clothes, or the simple warmth of a cup of tea in your hands - these are the micro-doses of regulation that, over time, rebuild the foundation of safety from the inside out.

Will this push/pull feeling ever go away completely?

The goal of this work is not necessarily the complete eradication of the push/pull impulse, but rather a fundamental change in your relationship to it. Recovery does not mean you will never again feel the urge to retreat or the desperate pull to cling. You develop the capacity to notice them as old, familiar sensations passing through, without being consumed or defined by them, creating the space to make a different choice.
